title: Ashoka's edicts across the Mauryan Empire
summary: In the middle of the third century BCE the Mauryan emperor Ashoka, appalled, on his own account, by the carnage of his conquest of Kalinga in around 261 BCE, ordered a programme of moral proclamations carved on rocks and polished sandstone pillars from Kandahar in modern Afghanistan to Karnataka in southern India. Written in Prakrit, Greek and Aramaic in a script called Brahmi and its regional variants, the edicts are the earliest surviving royal inscriptions in South Asia and the earliest expression of a policy of state-sponsored moral instruction anywhere in the ancient world.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Mauryan Empire had been founded around 322 BCE by Chandragupta Maurya, who from a base at Pataliputra on the Ganges (modern Patna) overthrew the last Nanda king and, within a decade, drove the Macedonian garrisons out of the Indus valley. In around 305 BCE, at the peace with Seleucus I Nicator, Chandragupta acquired the eastern satrapies west of the Indus, including modern Afghanistan and Baluchistan, in exchange for five hundred war elephants. His son Bindusara extended Mauryan authority into the Deccan. When Bindusara died, around 273 or 272 BCE, his son Ashoka, according to later Buddhist and Jain sources, secured the succession only after a four-year struggle with his brothers.

Ashoka's reign is conventionally dated from about 268 BCE to 232 BCE. For its first eight years he governed as his predecessors had, from Pataliputra, through a hierarchy of provincial capitals at Taxila in the north-west, Ujjain in the west, Suvarnagiri in the south and Tosali in the east. In his ninth regnal year, roughly 261 BCE, he undertook the war against Kalinga on the eastern coast (modern Odisha), the one major independent kingdom still outside the empire. His own account, given in his Major Rock Edict 13, states that 100,000 people were killed on the battlefield, many times that number perished afterwards, and 150,000 were deported. In the same edict he says that after seeing this suffering he felt profound remorse and turned to what he calls dhamma, the Prakrit form of Sanskrit dharma, and abandoned wars of conquest, adopting instead a policy of dhamma-vijaya, conquest by moral persuasion.

How far this conversion was as sudden and complete as the edicts represent is debated. Ashoka's Buddhist sympathies are clear from the pillars and inscriptions themselves; the Sri Lankan Pali chronicles (the Dipavamsa and Mahavamsa), composed centuries later, expand the story into a full conversion narrative, with the emperor becoming a lay Buddhist upasaka and, in some sources, briefly ordaining as a monk. What is beyond dispute is that from around 260 BCE Ashoka began the programme of inscriptions that has preserved his name. He tells us in one of the Minor Rock Edicts that for over a year after adopting dhamma he was not particularly zealous, but then, after spending more than a year with the sangha, the Buddhist community, he became so.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: The Major Pillar Edicts inscribed on the Delhi-Topra pillar, moved by Firuz Shah Tughluq to Delhi in 1356 CE.
image.credit: Ashoka's pillar, photo via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
The edicts fall into several groups. The Fourteen Major Rock Edicts, engraved on natural rock surfaces at some ten sites, form the earliest and most elaborate series, composed between roughly 260 and 255 BCE. They are found at Girnar in Gujarat, at Dhauli and Jaugada in Kalinga itself (with two revised versions omitting Edict 13's discussion of the war, evidently thought tactless in the conquered province), at Shahbazgarhi and Mansehra in the north-west (where the local Kharoshthi script is used instead of Brahmi), at Kalsi in the Doon valley, at Sopara north of Bombay, and at Erragudi and Yerragudi in Andhra Pradesh. The Kandahar bilingual inscription of about 260 BCE, on a rock at Chilzina outside modern Kandahar, is written in Greek and Aramaic for the Hellenised and Persian-speaking populations of the north-western frontier.

A second series, the Minor Rock Edicts, is shorter and more directly personal, addressed to Buddhist audiences and dealing largely with the emperor's own religious progress. It survives at more than a dozen sites including Bairat, Rupnath, Sasaram, Maski, Brahmagiri and Yerragudi. The Maski inscription, discovered in 1915, was the first to use Ashoka's personal name Devanampiya Piyadasi in combination with the name Ashoka; it settled a long-standing debate about whose reign the edicts belonged to.

A third series, the Seven Major Pillar Edicts, was engraved around 242 BCE, near the end of the reign, on polished sandstone monolithic pillars quarried at Chunar in modern Uttar Pradesh and shipped down the Ganges. Six sites preserve them: Delhi-Topra (the pillar now stands in New Delhi, moved by Firuz Shah Tughluq in 1356 CE), Delhi-Meerut (also relocated by Firuz Shah), Allahabad-Kosambi (moved to the Allahabad Fort), and the three in situ pillars at Lauriya Araraj, Lauriya Nandangarh and Rampurva in Bihar. The most famous pillar of all, the Sarnath lion capital, carried a shorter Minor Pillar Edict warning against schism in the Buddhist sangha; its four addorsed Asiatic lions and the dhamma wheel below them were adopted in 1950 as the state emblem of the Republic of India.

The content of the edicts is the substance of Ashoka's dhamma. He commands abstention from the killing of most animals; the planting of medicinal herbs and shade trees along the roads; the digging of wells; the appointment of officers called dhamma-mahamatas to oversee ethical instruction, to look after the welfare of prisoners, the sick and the elderly, and to protect religious minorities; the prohibition of costly festivals and animal sacrifice at the capital; the practice of tolerance among sects, since, as Rock Edict 12 puts it, the sects of other people all deserve reverence for one reason or another and by honouring them one honours one's own sect. Rock Edict 13 famously records embassies of dhamma to five named Hellenistic kings: Antiochus II of the Seleucids, Ptolemy II of Egypt, Antigonus II Gonatas of Macedon, Magas of Cyrene and Alexander of Epirus. Whether these missions were actually received in the western Mediterranean is unknown; no Greek source mentions them.

The inscriptions are written in a plain, direct first-person voice, opening most often with the formula, Thus speaks the Beloved of the Gods, the King Piyadasi. They are the first Indian inscriptions to survive in any substantial number, the first securely dated body of Prakrit prose, and the source of almost everything historians know about the internal life of the Mauryan Empire.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The Mauryan Empire did not long outlive Ashoka. He died around 232 BCE, and by 185 BCE the last Mauryan king, Brihadratha, had been killed by his general Pushyamitra Shunga, who founded the Shunga dynasty in northern India. The unity Chandragupta and Ashoka had assembled fragmented within two generations into a mosaic of regional kingdoms, and the specific administrative apparatus of the dhamma-mahamatas disappeared with the dynasty.

But two consequences endured. First, Ashoka's patronage transformed Buddhism from a regional monastic movement of the Ganges plain into a religion of empire and of long-distance mission. The Third Buddhist Council, according to Theravadin tradition, was held at Pataliputra during his reign under the presidency of the monk Moggaliputta Tissa, at which the canon was purified of heresy. From the council, Ashoka is said to have despatched Buddhist teachers to nine border regions, including Kashmir and Gandhara in the north-west, Suvarnabhumi (probably lower Burma or Thailand), and Sri Lanka, where his own son Mahinda and his daughter Sanghamitta converted the king Devanampiya Tissa and established the Mahavihara at Anuradhapura. The sapling of the Bodhi tree that Sanghamitta is said to have carried to Anuradhapura still stands there, or a lineal descendant of it, as the Sri Maha Bodhi.

Second, the physical infrastructure of Buddhist devotion took shape under Ashoka. Later tradition credits him with the construction of 84,000 stupas across the empire. Excavation confirms that a first stone rail was set around the Great Stupa at Sanchi during his reign, and that the enlargement of monastic sites at Bharhut, Sarnath, Kushinagar and Bodh Gaya was largely his work. The polished Chunar sandstone of the pillars and the sculptural style of the lion capitals reflect contact with the Achaemenid Persian tradition of Persepolis, transmitted through Bactria and Gandhara.

Within India itself the edicts were quickly forgotten. By the seventh century CE, when the Chinese pilgrim Xuanzang travelled through the Ganges plain, he could see many of Ashoka's pillars but could no longer read them; the Brahmi script had gone out of use. The pillar at Allahabad was reinscribed by the Gupta emperor Samudragupta in the fourth century CE with his own eulogy, and by the Mughal Jahangir in the seventeenth. The decipherment of Brahmi by James Prinsep of the Asiatic Society of Bengal in 1837, working from the Delhi pillar, restored to modern knowledge both the script and the emperor who had used it.

sections.heading: Legacy

image.caption: Brahmi script inscribed on the Ashokan pillar at Sarnath, deciphered by James Prinsep in 1837.

sections.body:
The rediscovery of Ashoka in the nineteenth century coincided with the growth of Indian national consciousness, and the emperor was rapidly enrolled as a founding figure of a specifically Indian civilisation of pluralism, non-violence and moral government. Jawaharlal Nehru placed the Sarnath lion capital on the reverse of the Republic's new state emblem and the twenty-four-spoke dhamma-cakra from the same capital on the centre of the national flag when independence was declared in August 1947. The lion capital itself, discovered by Friedrich Oertel in 1904 and 1905 during excavations for the Archaeological Survey of India, is now displayed in a purpose-built museum at Sarnath.

Outside India, Ashoka was almost unknown to European writers until the Prinsep decipherment. The pale, semi-legendary king Piyadasi mentioned by later Buddhist sources, and by the second-century CE writer Pausanias in a garbled reference to embassies from India, could not previously be attached to any actual reign. Once identified, Ashoka joined a small handful of pre-modern rulers, Marcus Aurelius, Akbar, and perhaps King Alfred of Wessex, cited by nineteenth- and twentieth-century historians as examples of the philosopher-king.

His edicts continue to have a life beyond the museum. The formula of Rock Edict 12, that all sects should be honoured under all circumstances, is quoted in modern arguments over Indian secularism and religious pluralism. UNESCO's inclusion of the Buddhist monuments at Sanchi (1989) and of the Mahabodhi Temple Complex at Bodh Gaya (2002) on the World Heritage List rests in part on their Ashokan foundations. And the Ashoka Chakra on the Indian flag is a rare instance of an ancient royal inscription becoming, without translation, the daily political symbol of a modern republic of a billion and a half people. The single body of texts he left behind is one of the most sustained attempts in history to answer, in the voice of a working ruler, the question of what a state exists to do.

worldContext.text: In the western Mediterranean the First Punic War between Rome and Carthage, waged over Sicily since 264 BCE, was in its final decade; it would end in 241 BCE with Roman naval victory at the Aegates Islands and the cession of Sicily. In mainland Greece the Antigonid kings of Macedon and the leagues of Aetolia and Achaea contested primacy, while the Stoics of the Painted Stoa at Athens under Zeno of Citium were consolidating a philosophy of duty and cosmopolitan citizenship that shared, at some distance, several of Ashoka's ethical concerns.

The Hellenistic successor kingdoms were established but chronically at war. Ptolemaic Egypt under Ptolemy II Philadelphus at Alexandria was funding the Library and the Museum, and had recently commissioned the Septuagint translation of the Hebrew Bible into Greek. The Seleucid Empire under Antiochus II ruled from Antioch to Bactria; his son and successor Antiochus III would fight the Second Syrian War with Ptolemy at exactly this period. In Judea, the small province was still Ptolemaic. The Nabataean Arabs were beginning to develop Petra as a caravan capital.

In China the Warring States were nearing their end. The state of Qin, reformed by Shang Yang more than a century earlier, was systematically absorbing its rivals; the final unification under Qin Shi Huang was still thirty years away. The philosopher Xunzi, teacher of the future Qin chancellor Li Si and of the Legalist theorist Han Fei, was writing at the Jixia Academy in Qi. On the northern frontier, the Xiongnu nomadic confederation was consolidating under a first line of shanyu rulers.

In Mesoamerica the Zapotec capital at Monte Alban in the Valley of Oaxaca was fully established as a regional power, its terraced platforms and carved stelae marking its dominion. In the Maya lowlands the great Preclassic centre of El Mirador in the Peten was at its peak, its triadic pyramids among the largest ever built in the ancient Americas. In the Andes the Chavín horizon had faded and the regional cultures of Paracas on the south Peruvian coast and early Nazca were producing their intricate textiles and ceramics.

In the middle Nile the Meroitic kingdom of Kush continued its royal cemetery of pyramids at Meroe, its rulers styling themselves qore and kandake and worshipping Amun, Isis and their own lion god Apedemak. Along the North African coast Carthage, exhausted by the Punic War with Rome, was consolidating its interior domains under generals of the Barcid family. Further west, Berber kingdoms in Numidia and Mauretania were absorbing Carthaginian material culture through trade.

In the western Pacific the descendants of the Lapita voyagers had by now settled Tonga, Samoa, Fiji, Vanuatu and the Marshall Islands. Ancestral Polynesian society, with its distinctive social hierarchies, canoe technology and horticultural repertoire of taro, breadfruit, coconut and pig, was consolidating; the great voyages that would carry Polynesians east to the Society Islands, the Marquesas and, later, Hawaii and Aotearoa still lay in the future. Aboriginal Australians continued the world's oldest unbroken cultural tradition.

figures.name: Ashoka Maurya
figures.role: Third Mauryan emperor, r. c. 268 to 232 BCE, whose edicts and Buddhist patronage transformed South Asian religious and political history

figures.name: Chandragupta Maurya
figures.role: Ashoka's grandfather and founder of the Mauryan Empire around 322 BCE

figures.name: Moggaliputta Tissa
figures.role: Buddhist monk who, according to the Sri Lankan Pali chronicles, presided over the Third Buddhist Council at Pataliputra during Ashoka's reign

figures.name: Mahinda
figures.role: Ashoka's son (or brother, in some sources), the monk who led the Buddhist mission to Sri Lanka in the mid-third century BCE

figures.name: James Prinsep
figures.role: Secretary of the Asiatic Society of Bengal who deciphered the Brahmi script of Ashoka's edicts in 1837, restoring them to modern knowledge

images.caption: The Lion Capital of Ashoka, from the pillar at Sarnath, c. 250 BCE, polished Chunar sandstone. The four addorsed Asiatic lions and the dhamma wheel below them are the source of the modern state emblem and flag of India. Sarnath Museum.
images.credit: Photograph by Yann Forget, 2010, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The Ashokan pillar at Vaishali in Bihar, one of the still-standing polished sandstone monoliths of the mid-third century BCE. Its single lion capital faces north, toward Kushinagar, where the Buddha entered parinirvana.
images.credit: Photograph by Bpilgrim, 2007, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Close-up of the Sarnath Lion Capital showing the four Asiatic lions, the abacus with animals and dhamma wheels, and the inverted lotus base. Mauryan period, c. 250 BCE.
images.credit: Photograph by Chrisi1964, via Wikimedia Commons
